The surviving record is interesting because it shows how even ordinary-looking books can acquire a charged political afterlife. As a for books banned during world war ii, see § world war ii (1939-1945)., it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in New Zealand. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: For books banned during World War II, see § World War II (1939-1945).
